summary: Gemma 4 is real (released March 31/April 2, 2026; E2B/E4B/12B/26B-MoE/31B) and — verified against Google's license page and HF model card — is genuinely Apache 2.0, a major change from Gemma 3's custom Terms of Use. However, the "SAM3 + Gemma" player-ID pattern the user saw is mostly hype-compression of a real Roboflow pattern: RF-DETR + SAM2 tracking + SigLIP team clustering + a small VLM (SmolVLM2, not Gemma) for jersey OCR — and in that very pipeline a fine-tuned ResNet-32 classifier (93%) beat the fine-tuned VLM (86%). SAM-family tracking runs 1–2 FPS on a T4 and would alone blow the 3.5 GPU-h gate, so keep RF-DETR + BoT-SORT. The published SOTA recipe for jersey numbers is a legibility classifier + torso crop + fine-tuned PARSeq (Apache-2.0, ~23M params): 87.45% tracklet accuracy on SoccerNet test, 92.85% challenge winner with an OCR ensemble — at <1 GPU-min/match versus 15–70 GPU-min for VLM candidates. Recommendation: adopt (team, number) as the long-gap anchor; build the PARSeq pipeline as the production reader; optionally use Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct (Apache-2.0, ~0.3–0.4 GPU-h/match) as a zero-shot baseline while collecting fine-tune data. Everything fits the cost gate; the bespoke route fits it ~100x more comfortably and is more accurate.
gemma_facts: Gemma 4 EXISTS as of June 2026. Releases page (ai.google.dev/gemma/docs/releases): initial release March 31, 2026 (blog announcement April 2, 2026) in E2B, E4B, 26B A4B (MoE) and 31B (dense) sizes; Gemma 4 12B "Unified" (dense, encoder-free multimodal) added June 3, 2026. Capabilities: native image+video input on all sizes (E2B/E4B also audio), variable-resolution vision with configurable image token budgets (70–1120 tokens/image), multilingual OCR listed as a capability, 128K context on edge models / up to 256K on larger ones. E4B is 4.5B effective params (8B with embeddings), "optimized for on-device". LICENSE — VERIFIED across three primary sources: (1) Google's announcement states verbatim "Gemma 4 is released under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license"; (2) ai.google.dev/gemma/apache_2 displays the standard Apache 2.0 text with no Gemma-specific modifications; (3) the HF model card google/gemma-4-e4b-it shows license: apache-2.0 and is NOT gated (no click-through terms required). Commercial SaaS use and redistribution are therefore permitted under standard Apache 2.0 grants ("perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable"). Caveat: Google still links a separate "Gemma Prohibited Use Policy" (last modified Feb 21, 2024) and an "Intended Use Statement" from the license page; for Apache-2.0 Gemma 4 these appear advisory rather than contractually binding (unlike Gemma 3, where the Terms of Use incorporated them), but note the policy explicitly lists "tracking or monitoring people without their consent" — adjacent enough to a player-tracking SaaS that legal should glance at it if Gemma is chosen. T4 fit: E4B ≈ 8–9GB FP16 weights, fits 16GB but runs at 4B-class speed (slow on Turing); 12B needs INT4 (~7GB) and T4 INT4 kernels are mediocre. Bottom line: license-clean, but no sports/jersey OCR evidence and not the fastest option for this job.
pattern_evidence: No credible published pipeline actually pairs SAM3 with Gemma 4 for player identification — the real, documented patterns are: (1) Roboflow basketball player identification (blog.roboflow.com/identify-basketball-players/): RF-DETR-S detection + SAM2 video tracking (temporal memory bank) + SigLIP embeddings + UMAP + K-means for team clustering + jersey number reading. The VLM's exact role: SmolVLM2 fine-tuned for jersey-number OCR on crops → 86% test accuracy; a fine-tuned ResNet-32 classifier beat it at 93%. Identity is confirmed by temporal voting: same prediction 3x in a row, sampling every 5 frames. Reported speed: 1–2 FPS on a T4 with SAM2 as the bottleneck — i.e., the SAM-as-tracker leg alone is ~10x your whole current pipeline's budget for a 90-min match. (2) Labellerr YOLO11-vs-SAM3 basketball system: SAM3 was benchmarked but rejected for the production loop (too slow; YOLO11 used for tracking); jersey numbers read by conventional OCR with a 30–50-frame voting buffer; Gemma is not used anywhere (Gemini only generated the article's diagrams); no accuracy reported; failure modes listed: motion blur, occlusion, lighting variation. (3) Roboflow sports-analytics workflows: RF-DETR detection + a Gemini 2.5 Pro VLM block for tactical structure analysis — the VLM does scene-level reasoning, not per-crop number OCR. (4) SAM3 tutorials (PyImageSearch Jan/Mar 2026, Ultralytics, Meta's SAM 3.1 blog): concept-prompted segmentation ("person" → instance IDs with streaming memory); identity resolution is explicitly left to OCR/embeddings downstream. SAM 3.1 Object Multiplex (Mar 27, 2026) speeds up multi-object video tracking but SAM3 remains a heavyweight model under a custom "SAM License" (commercial use allowed; military/ITAR-style restrictions; GitHub license shows NOASSERTION i.e. non-standard). Where the pattern breaks on non-broadcast footage: numbers are visible on only a "minimal subset of the whole tracklet" (SoccerNet task description), low-res thumbnails + high motion blur; in the analogous hockey dataset only 5% of player images were legible. Every working pipeline compensates with a legibility gate plus tracklet-level temporal voting — per-frame VLM reads are never trusted directly.
alternatives: Jersey-number evidence baseline: SoccerNet jersey-number task = 2,853 tracklets, tracklet-level accuracy, "low resolution, high motion blur". Best published: Koshkina & Elder (CVPRW 2024) ResNet34 legibility classifier + ViTPose torso crop + fine-tuned PARSeq → 87.45% SoccerNet test / 79.31% challenge / 91.4% hockey image-level; SoccerNet 2023 challenge winner ZZPMJ 92.85% (DBNet++ text detection + SVTR/SATRN/NRTR/ASTER OCR ensemble + majority voting), runner-up 90.95%. No published VLM beats these; the only quantified VLM result is Roboflow's fine-tuned SmolVLM2 at 86% vs fine-tuned ResNet-32 at 93%, and Qwen2-VL-based soccer research treats jersey reading as the failure mode requiring zoom-tool function calling. No source publishes an exact minimum crop resolution; the field handles small crops with legibility filtering + tracklet aggregation rather than per-frame guarantees (practical rule: filter crops where the torso region is small, and upscale 2–4x before reading). License-clean small-VLM candidates: (1) Qwen3-VL-2B/4B/8B-Instruct (Oct 2025, arXiv 2511.21631) — Apache-2.0 verified across the dense sizes, best-in-class OCR among small open VLMs, native dynamic resolution; the top zero-shot pick. WARNING: Qwen2.5-VL-3B is under the non-commercial Qwen Research License (verified on HF) — banned for your SaaS; Qwen2.5-VL-7B is Apache-2.0 but superseded. (2) SmolVLM2-256M/500M/2.2B — Apache-2.0; 500M needs only ~1.2GB VRAM; the one VLM with a proven jersey-OCR fine-tune recipe (86%); fastest VLM option. (3) Florence-2 base/large — MIT, 0.23B/0.77B, OCR-specialist prompt mode, but ~1s/image measured on T4, batch inference reportedly flaky, and it's not a chat model so structured attribute output is awkward. (4) InternVL3/3.5 (2B–8B) — MIT project license (built on Apache Qwen LLMs), license-clean and OCR-capable but heavier than Qwen3-VL-2B with no sports evidence. (5) PaliGemma 2 — still under the custom Gemma Terms of Use (gated) — NOT license-clean relative to the others; skip (ironically Gemma 4 is cleaner than PaliGemma 2). Versus a bespoke PARSeq fine-tune: PARSeq (baudm/parseq) is Apache-2.0, ~23M params, and is the documented accuracy leader at ~2 orders of magnitude less compute. One trap: the reference implementation repo mkoshkina/jersey-number-pipeline has NO license (NOASSERTION on GitHub) — do not vendor that code; reimplement the simple pipeline from Apache/BSD components (torchvision ResNet34 legibility gate, ViTPose or an upper-50%-of-bbox crop heuristic, PARSeq fine-tuned on SoccerNet jersey data).
throughput_estimate: Workload: ~1000 entities x 3 crops = 3000 calls at ~100 output tokens = ~300K output tokens (note: a constrained schema like "number: 7, color: red, role: outfield" is ~20 tokens — a free 5x decode saving). T4 = 65 TFLOPS FP16 / 320 GB/s / 16GB; no BF16 (cast to FP16), no FlashAttention-2 (use vLLM xformers backend). Estimates (batched, FP16 unless noted): SmolVLM2-500M: ~0.3–0.5s/crop single-stream, batch 16–32 fits trivially → 10–25 crops/s → 3000 crops in ~3–8 GPU-min (~0.1 GPU-h). Qwen3-VL-2B: 4GB weights → decode bandwidth-bound ~60–80 tok/s single-stream, batch 8–16 aggregate ~500–800 tok/s → ~8–12 min decode + ~5–8 min vision-encode/prefill → ~15–25 GPU-min (~0.3–0.4 GPU-h). Qwen3-VL-4B or Gemma 4 E4B (~8–9GB FP16): 2–3x slower → ~35–70 GPU-min (0.6–1.2 GPU-h); INT4/AWQ roughly halves this but Turing INT4 support is mediocre. Florence-2-large: ~1s/crop measured on T4 (Roboflow) with reported batch-inference issues → ~50 GPU-min; Florence-2-base ~15–25 min. Fine-tuned PARSeq + ResNet34 legibility gate: >200 crops/s → 3000 crops in <1 GPU-min; even reading 50 frames per tracklet (~150K crops) ≈ 10–15 GPU-min. Budget check vs the 3.5 GPU-h gate on top of the existing 1.2 GPU-h: every candidate fits (worst case Gemma 4 E4B ≈ 2.4 GPU-h total), but the ordering is PARSeq (<0.02 GPU-h) << SmolVLM2-500M (0.1) < Qwen3-VL-2B (0.3–0.4) < Florence-2-large (0.8) < 4B-class (0.6–1.2). The serverless caveat: if the VLM runs as a second model alongside the 1.2 GPU-h pipeline in the same job, add its weights to the 16GB budget (RF-DETR + SigLIP + 4GB Qwen3-VL-2B coexist fine; an 8–9GB E4B is tight).
recommendation: 1) Adopt (team, jersey number) as the long-gap identity anchor — correct instinct, it is exactly what the literature and working pipelines use. 2) Reject the "SAM3 + Gemma 4" framing: no real pipeline pairs them for identification; SAM-family tracking runs 1–2 FPS on a T4 (4–10+ GPU-h per match even subsampled — blows the gate), and your RF-DETR + BoT-SORT already does that job at 1.2 GPU-h. The pattern's real content is "per-crop number reader + temporal voting," which you can bolt on. 3) PRIMARY: fine-tune PARSeq (Apache-2.0, ~23M params) on the SoccerNet jersey dataset plus ~500 labeled crops from your own footage, gated by a ResNet34 legibility classifier on torso crops (pose-guided or upper-half-bbox heuristic). Published range 87–93% tracklet-level accuracy beats the only quantified VLM result (86% fine-tuned SmolVLM2); cost <1 GPU-min/match running in the SAME GPU job (total stays ~1.25 GPU-h, miles under the 3.5 gate). Reimplement the pipeline — do not vendor mkoshkina/jersey-number-pipeline (unlicensed). Keep SigLIP clustering for team/kit color and the detector class head for GK/ref/outfield — no VLM needed for attributes. 4) OPTIONAL week-one baseline while collecting fine-tune labels: Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct (Apache-2.0, 4GB FP16, ~0.3–0.4 GPU-h/match for 3000 crops) prompted for strict JSON {number, kit_color, role}; expect it to underperform fine-tuned PARSeq on blurry crops — treat it as scaffolding and a labeling assistant, not the destination. Gemma 4 E4B is license-clean (genuine Apache 2.0) but slower on a T4, has zero jersey-OCR evidence, and Google's prohibited-use policy mentions tracking people without consent — pick Qwen3-VL and skip the ambiguity. 5) Merge policy: merge entities only on (team-cluster, number) with ≥3 temporally consistent reads from ≥2 distinct tracklets, never across team clusters (two teams both have a #7); expect numbers legible on only a minority of tracklets, so number anchors collapse clusters and SigLIP embeddings bridge the illegible remainder. Expected lift: ~995 entities → ~30–60 (22 starters + subs + GKs + officials), with the residual being short illegible fragments. 6) Validation: hold out 2 full matches of your non-broadcast footage; label ~200 tracklet→player ground truths; measure (a) tracklet number accuracy on the legible subset (target ≥85%), (b) merge precision (ship gate: >99% — one wrong merge corrupts two player timelines and is worse than a miss), (c) final entity count and identity coverage minutes. If legibility on your camera angle is <10% of tracklets, add a 2–4x super-resolution upscale before the reader and re-measure before considering any larger model.
sources: https://arxiv.org/html/2405.13896v1 (Koshkina & Elder, A General Framework for Jersey Number Recognition, CVPRW 2024)
https://github.com/facebookresearch/sam3 (custom SAM License, NOASSERTION via GitHub API)
